title: AI增强化学成像与蛋白质相互作用动态监测验证
domain: AI-enhanced chemical imaging methods for dynamic monitoring of protein-protein interactions
objective: 验证 ZhiZhi → TanXi → MingLi 从文献检索、知识图谱、gap发现到可验证假设生成的闭环。

papergraph.title: Self-Supervised Deep-Learning Denoising for X-Ray Fluorescence Microscopy with Multi-Element Detectors
papergraph.citation: Rodion Shishkov et al. (2025) Self-Supervised Deep-Learning Denoising for X-Ray Fluorescence Microscopy with Multi-Element Detectors doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-lsxpc
papergraph.abstract: Scanning emission-based microscopies, such as X-ray fluorescence (XRF) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, offer nanometer-scale chemical maps, but suffer from long acquisition times and radiation damage. Lower-flux and shorter dwell time scans mitigate this problem, but the resulting signal loss can only partially be compensated by adding detector elements, a strategy limited by the instrument’s geometry and cost. We introduce an unsupervised machine learning pipeline that recovers signal by exploiting the intrinsic redundancy in the data captured by multi-element detectors. Inspired by the Noise2Noise approach, we train a deep convolutional neural network directly on the multiple, statistically independent noisy images collected by individual detector elements, without requiring clean training targets. Tested on a resolution target and a cryogenically fixed biological cell, the method markedly improved signal-to-noise ratios over classical filters while preserving spatial resolution and elemental quantification even for small images. To our knowledge, this is the first ML-based denoiser demonstrated for XRF. This approach reduces the dependence of image quality on photon dose and enables rapid, low-dose chemical imaging. It is readily transferable to any modality that records parallel, noise-independent views of the same sample.
strengths: markedly improved signal-to-noise ratios over classical filters
preserves spatial resolution and elemental quantification
first ML-based denoiser demonstrated for XRF
reduces dependence of image quality on photon dose
readily transferable to any modality recording parallel, noise-independent views
improvements: extend to other modalities recording parallel, noise-independent views
papergraph.scenario: X-ray fluorescence (XRF) microscopy with multi-element detectors
papergraph.contribution: First self-supervised deep-learning denoising pipeline for XRF microscopy that improves SNR without clean targets while preserving spatial resolution and elemental quantification.
papergraph.limitation: Strategy of adding detector elements is limited by instrument geometry and cost.

papergraph.title: Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester
papergraph.citation: Takanori Matsumaru et al. (2025) Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-w7lbt
papergraph.abstract: Mincle, a member of the C-type lectin receptor (CLR) family, detects various glycolipids and glycerolipids, such as trehalose dimycolate (TDM) from Mycobacterium tuberculosis, leading to the activation of the innate immune system. In this study, we developed new fluorescence-labeled molecular probes, N-NBD-labeled TDE and O-NBD-labeled TDE, based on the structure of trehalose diester to elucidate the intracellular behavior of Mincle and its ligands. N-NBD-labeled TDE was prepared for the ligand analysis, and O-NBD-labeled TDE was specifically designed to label Mincle by Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling. Live-cell imaging analysis using these probes revealed that N-NBD-labeled TDE internalizes into the cell in a Mincle-dependent manner. Furthermore, imaging analysis using O-NBD-labeled TDE successfully detected Mincle in cells in a Mincle expression-dependent manner.
papergraph.scenario: intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligands
papergraph.contribution: Development and application of two novel fluorescence-labeled trehalose diester probes enabling live-cell visualization of Mincle–ligand interactions, with N-NBD-TDE reporting ligand uptake and O-NBD-TDE enabling Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling of Mincle.

papergraph.title: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis
papergraph.citation: Pia Lopez et al. (2024) Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-kl8mp-v2
papergraph.abstract: Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) is an emerging tool to characterize ongoing chemical reactions in synthetic chemistry and catalysis. Initially applied to biological systems, FLIM now reveals spatially resolved chemical reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes that accompany ongoing reactions. FLIM combines the advantage of environmental sensitivity with high signal sensitivity (as sensitive as single molecules) and has the key ability to operate under synthetic conditions (e.g., high concentrations of reagents, in organic solvents, under ambient temperature and pressure, in opaque mixtures, and in multiphasic systems). Chemical reactions inherently induce changes in the reaction medium, neighboring compounds, surface compositions, and/or the bonding structure of the compounds involved, resulting in environmental changes. The FLIM methods recently developed harness and interpret these changes in ways that lead to characterizing compounds and enhancing mechanistic understanding. Here, current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered, common factors influencing fluorescence lifetime in chemical systems are discussed in a tutorial format, and seven research case studies are strategically analyzed—chosen to highlight how FLIM provided complementary information to understand chemical reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, roles of reagents, and catalyst behaviors. These data and insights obtained from FLIM assist the rational design and optimization of synthetic and catalytic methods.
strengths: high signal sensitivity (as sensitive as single molecules)
ability to operate under synthetic conditions (e.g., high concentrations of reagents, in organic solvents, under ambient temperature and pressure, in opaque mixtures, and in multiphasic systems)
improvements: Abstract: Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) is an emerging tool to characterize ongoing chemical reactions in synthetic chemistry and catalysis. Initially applied to biological systems, FLIM now reveals spatially resolved chemical reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes...[truncated]
papergraph.contribution: FLIM provides complementary information to understand chemical reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, roles of reagents, and catalyst behaviors, assisting rational design and optimization of synthetic and catalytic methods.
papergraph.limitation: Current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered.

papergraph.citation: Pia Lopez et al. (2024) Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-kl8mp
papergraph.abstract: Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) is an emerging tool to characterize ongoing chemical reactions in synthetic chemistry and catalysis. Initially applied to biological systems, FLIM now reveals spatially resolved chemical reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes that accompany ongoing reactions. FLIM combines the advantage of environmental sensitivity with high signal sensitivity (as sensitive as single molecules) and has the key ability to operate under synthetic conditions (e.g., high concentrations of reagents, in organic solvents, under ambient temperature and pressure, in opaque mixtures, and in multiphasic systems). Chemical reactions inherently induce changes in the reaction medium, neighboring compounds, surface compositions, and/or the bonding structure of the compounds involved, resulting in environmental changes. keynotes.title: Self-Supervised Deep-Learning Denoising for X-Ray Fluorescence Microscopy with Multi-Element Detectors

keynote.title: Self-Supervised Deep-Learning Denoising for X-Ray Fluorescence Microscopy with Multi-Element Detectors
keynote.core_problem: Long acquisition times and radiation damage in X-ray fluorescence (XRF) microscopy; trade-off between photon dose, signal quality, and spatial/elemental fidelity when using low-flux or short-dwell scans.
contributions: First ML-based denoiser demonstrated for XRF microscopy
Unsupervised deep-learning pipeline exploiting intrinsic redundancy across multi-element detector outputs
Noise2Noise-inspired training without clean ground-truth targets
Demonstrated preservation of spatial resolution and elemental quantification under low-dose conditions
Enables rapid, low-dose chemical imaging with reduced dependence on photon dose
methods: Unsupervised deep convolutional neural network trained on statistically independent noisy images from multiple detector elements
experiments_or_evidence: Tested on a cryogenically fixed biological cell
Marked improvement in signal-to-noise ratio over classical filters
Preservation of spatial resolution and elemental quantification even for small images
assumptions: Noisy images from different detector elements are statistically independent
Intrinsic redundancy exists across multi-element detector views of the same sample
The underlying signal is consistent across detector elements despite independent noise realizations
limitations: Strategy of adding detector elements is limited by instrument geometry and cost
Method relies on availability of multi-element detectors capturing parallel, noise-independent views

gap_signals.text: Method relies on availability of multi-element detectors capturing parallel, noise-independent views
Strategy of adding detector elements is limited by instrument geometry and cost
To our knowledge, this is the first ML-based denoiser demonstrated for XRF.
It is readily transferable to any modality that records parallel, noise-independent views of the same sample.

important_claims.claim: The method markedly improved signal-to-noise ratios over classical filters while preserving spatial resolution and elemental quantification even for small images.
important_claims.evidence: Tested on a resolution target and a cryogenically fixed biological cell.

important_claims.claim: This approach reduces the dependence of image quality on photon dose and enables rapid, low-dose chemical imaging.
important_claims.evidence: Lower-flux and shorter dwell time scans mitigate radiation damage; method recovers signal without clean targets.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — provides a generalizable unsupervised denoising framework for any parallel, noise-independent multi-view imaging modality; eliminates need for ground-truth data or synthetic noise models.

keynotes.title: Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester

keynote.title: Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester
keynote.core_problem: Lack of tools to dynamically visualize intracellular behavior of Mincle and its ligands in live cells.
contributions: Development of two novel fluorescence-labeled trehalose diester (TDE) probes: N-NBD-labeled TDE for ligand tracking and O-NBD-labeled TDE for Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling of Mincle.
Demonstration of Mincle-dependent internalization of N-NBD-labeled TDE in live cells.
Successful detection of Mincle expression in live cells using O-NBD-labeled TDE in a Mincle expression-dependent manner.
methods: Chemical synthesis of N-NBD-labeled TDE and O-NBD-labeled TDE.
Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling strategy using O-NBD-labeled TDE.
Mincle-dependence validation via comparison across cell types with varying Mincle expression levels.
experiments_or_evidence: Live-cell imaging showing internalization of N-NBD-labeled TDE is abolished or reduced in Mincle-deficient cells.
Fluorescence signal from O-NBD-labeled TDE correlates with Mincle expression level across tested cell lines.
assumptions: O-NBD-labeled TDE selectively labels Mincle via specific binding and Turn-ON fluorescence activation.
N-NBD-labeled TDE behaves as a functional ligand analog retaining Mincle-binding and internalization properties.
Observed fluorescence signals reflect true subcellular localization and not artifacts (e.g., non-specific binding, probe degradation).

gap_signals.text: Limited understanding of real-time intracellular trafficking and spatial distribution of Mincle–ligand complexes.
Prior absence of live-cell-compatible, Mincle-specific fluorescent probes enabling dynamic intracellular tracking.

important_claims.claim: N-NBD-labeled TDE internalizes into cells in a Mincle-dependent manner.
important_claims.evidence: Live-cell imaging shows internalization is absent or diminished in Mincle-deficient cells.

important_claims.claim: O-NBD-labeled TDE detects Mincle in a Mincle expression-dependent manner.
important_claims.evidence: Fluorescence signal intensity from O-NBD-labeled TDE scales with Mincle expression level across cell lines.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides chemically defined, live-cell-compatible probes for studying CLR biology; enables future work on Mincle trafficking, ligand engagement kinetics, and host–pathogen interactions involving trehalose-containing ligands.

keynotes.title: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis

keynote.title: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis
keynote.core_problem: Characterizing ongoing chemical reactions and catalytic processes in situ with spatial resolution, especially under synthetically relevant conditions where conventional analytical tools fail.
contributions: Positioning FLIM as an emerging tool for synthetic chemistry and catalysis beyond its traditional biological applications
Highlighting FLIM's ability to provide spatially resolved characterization of reaction species and physiochemical changes
Providing a tutorial on factors influencing fluorescence lifetime in chemical systems
Analyzing seven case studies demonstrating FLIM's complementary role in elucidating reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, reagent roles, and catalyst behaviors
Connecting FLIM-derived insights to rational design and optimization of synthetic and catalytic methods
methods: Interpretation of environmental sensitivity of fluorescence lifetime
Tutorial-style analysis of lifetime-influencing factors (e.g., solvent polarity, viscosity, pH, molecular interactions, quenching)
experiments_or_evidence: Seven strategically selected research case studies
Demonstration of FLIM operation under synthetic conditions: high reagent concentrations, organic solvents, ambient temperature/pressure, opaque mixtures, multiphasic systems
assumptions: Fluorescent probes or intrinsic fluorophores report local environmental changes linked to reaction progress
Fluorescence lifetime changes are interpretable in complex chemical environments
Spatial lifetime heterogeneity correlates meaningfully with chemical heterogeneity (e.g., phase boundaries, catalyst localization, intermediate accumulation)
limitations: Limitations of FLIM method are discussed (but specific limitations not enumerated in provided text)

gap_signals.text: Limitations of FLIM method are discussed (but specific limitations not enumerated in provided text)
Conventional tools fail in opaque, multiphasic, or high-concentration synthetic conditions — FLIM addresses this by operating under such conditions.
Originally applied to biological systems, now extended to synthetic chemistry — suggesting cross-domain transfer and unmet needs in chemical reaction monitoring.
FLIM is 'an emerging tool' for synthetic chemistry and catalysis — implying limited adoption and methodological standardization in these domains.

important_claims.claim: FLIM operates under synthetically relevant conditions including high reagent concentrations, organic solvents, ambient temperature and pressure, opaque mixtures, and multiphasic systems.
important_claims.evidence: Stated directly in abstract as a 'key ability'.

important_claims.claim: Chemical reactions induce environmental changes (in medium, neighboring compounds, surfaces, bonding structures) that FLIM harnesses and interprets.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly described in abstract as the foundational principle enabling FLIM's application.

important_claims.claim: FLIM provides complementary information to understand reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, reagent roles, and catalyst behaviors.
important_claims.evidence: Supported by analysis of seven research case studies.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High reuse value as a conceptual and methodological bridge between optical microscopy and reaction engineering; enables spatially resolved, label-free (or minimally invasive) monitoring of reactions in challenging chemical environments — applicable to catalyst screening, reaction optimization, and mechanistic validation.

keynotes.title: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis

keynote.title: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis
keynote.core_problem: Characterizing spatially resolved chemical reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes during ongoing synthetic chemical reactions and catalysis.
contributions: Demonstrates FLIM as a tool for spatially resolved characterization of chemical reactions under synthetically relevant conditions.
Provides a tutorial on factors influencing fluorescence lifetime in chemical systems.
Analyzes seven case studies showing how FLIM delivers complementary mechanistic insights (e.g., intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, catalyst behavior).
Discusses advantages and limitations of FLIM for chemistry applications.
methods: Interpretation of fluorescence lifetime changes induced by environmental perturbations (e.g., polarity, viscosity, pH, binding, quenching).
experiments_or_evidence: Seven research case studies illustrating FLIM's utility in synthetic chemistry and catalysis.
Analysis of FLIM-derived data on reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, reagent roles, and catalyst behaviors.
assumptions: Fluorescence lifetime is sensitive to local physicochemical environment changes induced by chemical reactions.
Fluorophores (endogenous or exogenous) are present or can be introduced without perturbing reaction outcomes.
Lifetime signals can be disentangled from confounding photophysical effects (e.g., photobleaching, scattering) in complex reaction media.
limitations: Limitations of FLIM method are discussed (exact limitations not specified in provided text).

gap_signals.text: Limitations of FLIM method are discussed (exact limitations not specified in provided text).
Initially applied to biological systems — suggests underutilization in non-biological chemical contexts.
Requires tutorial on lifetime-influencing factors — indicates need for domain-specific photophysical literacy among chemists.
FLIM is an emerging tool — implies limited adoption and standardized protocols in synthetic chemistry/catalysis.

important_claims.claim: FLIM operates under synthetic conditions (e.g., high reagent concentrations, organic solvents, ambient T/P, opaque/multiphasic mixtures).
important_claims.evidence: Stated directly in abstract as a 'key ability'.

important_claims.claim: FLIM reveals spatially resolved chemical reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes.
FLIM provides complementary information to understand reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, reagent roles, and catalyst behaviors.
important_claims.evidence: Supported by analysis of seven strategically chosen case studies.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — enables spatiotemporal, label-free (or minimally invasive), and environmentally sensitive monitoring of reactions in realistic synthetic environments; supports mechanistic hypothesis generation and rational optimization of catalytic systems.
